utterances: And they report that the idea that they were being watched by friends and family members and their colleagues and companions was destructive to their family relationships, to political and intellectual development, to the artistic communities, merely because the bonds of trust had been so eroded.

And I think when we consider using informants, not - informant systems and surveillance - not just to monitor driving, anonymous, or other extremely public, minor offenses, but more important life activities, that's where using informants becomes potentially socially destructive public policy.
Dez thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.
Bye-bye.
And Alexandra Natapoff, thank you for your time today.
We appreciate it.
My pleasure.
Thank you.
Alexandra Natapoff is an associate professor of law at Loyola Law School in Los Angeles.
She joined us on the phone from her home in Los Angeles.
When we come back from a short break, we're going to talk about the best hacks, upgrades, and downloads to fix your folks computer while you're visiting back home over the holidays.
If you have a question or a tip give us a call, 800-989-8255, 800-989-TALK.
You can also send us an e-mail if your parent's computer's working, talk@npr.org.
I'm Neal Conan.
We'll be back after the break.
It's the TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
Some immigrants in this country say they have been treated differently during and after the recent presidential election.
Let's meet one of them.
Tenzin Dorjee is a naturalized U.S.
citizen.
He runs a successful restaurant and also an arts festival in upstate New York.
In his descriptions of the harassment he's faced, you will hear language that you might find offensive.
North Country Public Radio's Zach Hirsch reports that his experience has triggered a powerful community response.
Tenzin Dorjee grew up in Bhutan and for the past nine years, he's lived in Plattsburgh, N.Y., a tiny city near the U.S.-Canada border.
Dorjee said he's been singled out a few times over the years, but nothing like what he experienced the day after the election.
There's a couple of guys standing next to a couple of trucks.
And, you know, that's when they say hey, chink, get the F out of my country.
Go back to where you came from.
And I just smiled at them, and then it happened again.
Just a few steps away, he said two other people used the same racial slur.
Later, he was out running errands when a car cut him off.
Dorjee said the driver flashed him the middle finger.
A few days after that, Dorjee's car was vandalized while it was parked outside his house.
He was really shaken up.
I was just scared for my kids and my family.
On Facebook, he wrote a long, emotional post where he said he decided to become a gun owner.
A weapon of destruction is my only safeguard against the hate that has been directed against the color of my skin and political beliefs.
Being peaceful is a huge part of Dorjee's identity.
He's a practicing Buddhist, but he felt like he was out of options so he planned to get a shotgun for the house and get a license to carry a pistol.
He said it was a dark, low moment, but then he heard from friends.
I've had community members come up to me and say, Tenzin, you know, we are behind you all the way.
The support kept pouring in.
His phones have been off the hook with people calling and messaging just to tell Dorjee they've got his back.
And at a local film festival, the emcee Michael Devine surprised Dorjee with an award just for being a good person.
Tenzin, who's here tonight with our first annual Mandela award...
(APPLAUSE)
The Army has a total of 39 brigade combat teams.
Each one is made up of about roughly 3,500 troops.
There are 15 in Iraq now and the president is sending an additional six, which will mean that more than half of the Army's fighting strength will be in Iraq by the middle of this year.
Well, proponents of the surge, including Senator John McCain, have lobbied for a much larger, longer-term boost.
Is that even an option given what you're saying about the military deployment?
And is there a reason that the president isn't asking for even more?
Yeah, there is, Farai.
And the main reason is because the Joint Chiefs, essentially the people who are in charge of this branch of the armed forces, are saying, this is the maximum we can give you.
And now we've heard a lot about how the Joint Chiefs have been a bit reluctant to press for an increase.
They know that their services are stretched, particularly the Army and the Marines.
Their hands are tied in many ways.
They can't draw from the National Guard and the reserve to the extent to which they would like to.
So the Joint Chiefs essentially told the president, look, the limit, the maximum number we can give you is 21,500, and that's the number the president went with.
Is the president really going to set a date or a standard by which to measure the success of the surge?

You know, he didn't mention that in the speech last night, and that's a little bit odd because of course the president's main argument last night was that the surge - or he didn't use the word surge - but that the troop increase would bring about security and ultimately that will enable the Iraqi government to take the necessary measures to bring about stability in the country.
He didn't give a sense of how long the troops will be there, and he didn't essentially give any timeline as to when the American public can expect to see some progress in Iraq.
So how is Washington reacting to the president's speech?
Almost overwhelmingly negatively.
I mean, even members of the president's own party have been reluctant to back this in any sort of serious way.
The Republican minority leader in the House, John Boehner, he essentially said last night that we should wait and see what the details of the plan are.
I mean he didn't come out saying, yes, I'm backing an increase.
There are very few members of the House or the Senate who are doing the same thing.
And clearly, the overwhelming majority of Democrats in both chambers very strongly opposed to this.
They are going to call for a vote, a symbolic vote, really, to allow those members to show their opposition to this decision.
Well, Guy Raz, thanks so much.
Thank you, Farai.
NPR's Guy Raz covers defense issues.
He joined us from the Pentagon.
This is WEEKEND EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Ari Shapiro.
Rachel Martin is away.
Brazilians go to the polls today at the end of a dramatic campaign season.
One candidate died in a plane crash.
Presidential incumbent Dilma Rousseff is a former political prisoner and cancer survivor.
Pitted against her is Marina Silva, a popular environmentalist who grew up in the Amazon.
Also running is the grandson of a beloved political figure.
NPR's Lourdes Garcia-Navarro joins us now from Sao Palo.
Good morning.
Good morning.
So the polls are open.
Describe where you are and what you're seeing.
I'm at a polling station in the west of the city in a neighborhood called Morumbi.
It's at a school.
And in this country, not only is voting obligatory, it's totally electronic.
No paper trail.
So everything is done on a computer screen.
And people are filing in and out pretty quickly.
As we mentioned, this has been a dramatic campaign season.
Run us through some of the highlights.
In mid-August, the man who was running in third place at that point for president, Eduardo Campos in a plane crash.
And that upended the race.
His running mate, Marina Silva, then took the top spot and surged in the polls.
She has a very dramatic story.
She grew up poor and illiterate in the Amazon.
She's of Afro-Brazilian decent, a first in this country for someone running for president.
And she worked as a maid to put herself through school.
Suddenly, she seemed like the woman to beat.
But the incumbent, Dilma Rousseff, and her party went on a very negative offensive.
And now in a few short weeks, we've seen it turn around again.
And Dilma is on top.
Tell us more about that surge.
Why did the incumbent come back apart from the negative campaigning you mentioned?
It's been a combination of factors.
Here in Brazil, the bigger party gets more airtime so Rousseff's party has been blanketing the airwaves.
And I think people are worried about losing what they've gained under Rousseff's party.
Even though Brazil is now in bad economic shape, the last decade has seen impressive growth and the expansion of social policies that have really benefited the poor.
I could imagine some American listeners thinking why does it matter to me who wins the Brazilian presidential election.
Remind us what's at stake on the global stage here.
Yeah.
This is the sixth largest economy in the world.
A change in leadership here could really signal a shift in foreign policy and certainly a shift in monetary policy.
Brazil under Rousseff and her predecessor have been very protectionist, and the markets really want to see a change in direction.
Also, this is where the biggest chunk of the rain forest is; the world's lungs as they are called.
And finally, this will impact the U.S.
Rousseff's government has had a very rocky relationship with the United States, and the other candidates are much more pro-U.S.
That's NPR's Lourdes Garcia-Navarro speaking with us from a polling station in the Morimbi neighborhood of Sao Paulo Brazil.
Thanks, Lulu.
You're welcome.
It is a question asked so often around the world - is an election truly fair?
That question will be asked yet again when people in Pakistan vote next week.
NPR's Diaa Hadid explains why.
There's a dusty field in the town of Narowal, near the border with India, where a political party's holding an election rally.
The party's green and red flags snap in the wind.
Men stand on chairs, straining to see the person they want for prime minister, Imran Khan.
